title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Karim Lane, who keeps bees on the roof of a city building.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're talking to Karim Lane, who keeps bees on the roof of a city building. Karim, keeping bees in the city sounds unusual. Why did you start? || Guest: It began through my job at an office, not through farming. Our building had a flat roof that nobody used, and we wanted a green project for staff. I read about city bees and visited a local beekeeper. [20]I started because the empty roof seemed like a good place to help nature[/20]. I did not expect to love it so much, but watching the first bees arrive was wonderful. || Interviewer: What was difficult when you were starting out? || Guest: My family thought the bees would be dangerous, and I also worried about that at first. But bees usually stay calm if you move slowly. [21]The thing that surprised me most was how quiet the roof felt, even with thousands of bees[/21]. You can hear traffic below, but near the hives there is just a gentle sound. It is strangely peaceful in the middle of the city. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most about it now? || Guest: We do get honey, but that is not the main point. The best result is what happens to people in the office. [22]Staff who never spoke to each other now meet on the roof to check the hives[/22]. Someone from accounts may stand next to someone from reception, and they both ask questions. The bees have made the workplace friendlier, which was a lovely surprise. || Interviewer: Have you changed the way you work since then? || Guest: The weather is the main difficulty. People imagine city bees have problems finding flowers, but parks and gardens are close to us. However, [23]strong wind on the roof can make it unsafe to open the hives[/23]. I have to check the forecast carefully and sometimes cancel a visit. The bees are fine, but we must not risk dropping equipment from the roof. || Interviewer: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work? || Guest: I have learnt to explain the project better. At first, I used too many technical words, and some visitors looked confused. [24]Now I show people the equipment first, before I talk about how bees live[/24]. Holding a frame or seeing the protective suit makes the ideas easier. Then they can understand why bees need space, food and careful handling. || Interviewer: And what are your plans for the future? || Guest: This year we are adding more plants around the building entrance. Next, [25]I want to invite local school groups to see the hives from a safe viewing area[/25]. They would not touch the bees, but they could watch me work and learn why insects are important. I think children remember nature better when they can see it close to home. || Interviewer: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar? || Guest: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like city beekeeping, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal. || Interviewer: Thank you, that was really interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're talking to Karim Lane, who keeps bees on the roof of a city building. Karim, keeping bees in the city sounds unusual. Why did you start?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It began through my job at an office, not through farming. Our building had a flat roof that nobody used, and we wanted a green project for staff. I read about city bees and visited a local beekeeper. I started because the empty roof seemed like a good place to help nature. I did not expect to love it so much, but watching the first bees arrive was wonderful.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult when you were starting out?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: My family thought the bees would be dangerous, and I also worried about that at first. But bees usually stay calm if you move slowly. The thing that surprised me most was how quiet the roof felt, even with thousands of bees. You can hear traffic below, but near the hives there is just a gentle sound. It is strangely peaceful in the middle of the city.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy most about it now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We do get honey, but that is not the main point. The best result is what happens to people in the office. Staff who never spoke to each other now meet on the roof to check the hives. Someone from accounts may stand next to someone from reception, and they both ask questions. The bees have made the workplace friendlier, which was a lovely surprise.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have you changed the way you work since then?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The weather is the main difficulty. People imagine city bees have problems finding flowers, but parks and gardens are close to us. However, strong wind on the roof can make it unsafe to open the hives. I have to check the forecast carefully and sometimes cancel a visit. The bees are fine, but we must not risk dropping equipment from the roof.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I have learnt to explain the project better. At first, I used too many technical words, and some visitors looked confused. Now I show people the equipment first, before I talk about how bees live. Holding a frame or seeing the protective suit makes the ideas easier. Then they can understand why bees need space, food and careful handling.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: And what are your plans for the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: This year we are adding more plants around the building entrance. Next, I want to invite local school groups to see the hives from a safe viewing area. They would not touch the bees, but they could watch me work and learn why insects are important. I think children remember nature better when they can see it close to home.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like city beekeeping, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Thank you, that was really interesting.

questions.question: Why did Karim first put bees on the roof?

options.A: He wanted to produce honey for his office.
options.B: He had worked on farms when he was young.
options.C: He wanted to use an empty space to help nature.

questions.explanation: Karim says he started because the empty roof seemed like a good place to help nature.

questions.question: What surprised Karim about the roof?

options.B: It was too hot for the bees.

questions.explanation: Karim says he was surprised by how quiet and peaceful the roof felt.

questions.question: What does Karim say the bees have done for the office?

options.A: They have produced a lot of honey.
options.B: They have helped staff get to know each other.
options.C: They have made the building famous locally.

questions.explanation: Karim says staff who never spoke now meet on the roof to check the hives.

questions.question: What problem does Karim mention?

options.A: There are not enough flowers nearby.
options.C: Wind can make roof visits unsafe.

questions.explanation: Karim says strong wind on the roof can make it unsafe to open the hives.

questions.question: How has Karim changed his talks to visitors?

options.A: He shows the equipment before explaining ideas.
options.B: He makes the talks much shorter.
options.C: He gives visitors written information first.

questions.explanation: Karim now shows people the equipment first, before talking about how bees live.

questions.question: What would Karim like to do next?

options.A: sell plants at the building entrance
options.B: let school groups watch the bees safely
options.C: move some hives to a local school

questions.explanation: Karim wants to invite school groups to see the hives from a safe viewing area.
